candidate_problems.text: What is the right formal criterion for when a goal-capability pairing is reflectively stable under self-modification, ontological change, and environmental feedback? The conjecture hinges on replacing bare logical possibility with a stronger notion like structural closure or self-maintenance, but it leaves open how to define and measure that notion across architectures rather than by intuition.

Which aspects of advanced capability create intrinsic selection pressures on terminal goals, versus merely increasing the engineering cost of preserving arbitrary goals? This is the core unresolved tension: does greater capability genuinely narrow the space of sustainable goals, or can sufficiently general scaffolding preserve near-arbitrary goals without changing the agent’s effective objective?

How much of the apparent coupling between goals and capability is architecture-relative rather than universal? The conjecture may depend on assumptions inherited from current optimizer-like, model-based agents; an open question is whether alternative designs could realize highly capable systems with very different stable goal structures, preserving more orthogonality than the conjecture allows.
